items.q: What does an increase in entropy of a system correspond to?
items.correct: An increase in the number of ways energy and particles can be arranged — typically a more dispersed/'disordered' state.
items.misconception: Entropy is just a measure of how messy a system looks visually.
items.random_wrong: Entropy measures how much energy a system contains in total.
items.why: The 'disorder' analogy is overused: students take it literally (mess vs tidy) instead of as a count of microstates.

items.q: Why can an endothermic reaction still be spontaneous?
items.correct: If the entropy increase (TΔS) is large enough to outweigh the positive ΔH, ΔG can still be negative.
items.misconception: Endothermic reactions are never spontaneous because they require energy input.
items.random_wrong: All endothermic reactions are spontaneous at room temperature.
items.why: Students equate 'exothermic' with 'spontaneous' from GCSE, missing the role of entropy in Gibbs free energy.

items.q: For a process to be spontaneous, what must happen to the total entropy of the universe?
items.correct: It must increase (ΔS_total > 0) — the system's entropy alone can decrease as long as the surroundings' entropy increases more.
items.misconception: The entropy of the system must increase for the process to be spontaneous.
items.random_wrong: The entropy of the surroundings must stay constant for the process to be spontaneous.
items.why: Students apply the second law only to the system, missing that what counts is system + surroundings (the whole universe) — which is why exothermic reactions can be spontaneous even with ΔS_system < 0.
The answer examiners want is: H2O(g). “H2O(l)” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
The answer examiners want is: ΔS_total > 0. “ΔS_system < 0” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Exact answer: 671. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
